sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me compare previous_frame to current_frame to see what changed after the mouse click.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
prev_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in previous_frame.segmentation['nodes']}
curr_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']}
for nid in curr_nodes:
    if nid in prev_nodes:
        pn = prev_nodes[nid]
        cn = curr_nodes[nid]
        if pn['boundary'] != cn['boundary'] or pn['color'] != cn['color']:
            print("Node %d changed: color %s->%s, boundary %s->%s" % (nid, pn['color'], cn['color'], pn['boundary'], cn['boundary']))
print("No changes found")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
The changes are in the bottom bar (rows 54-63). Node 38 (G - dark gray) and Node 39 (W - white) changed slightly. This looks like a timer/progress bar at the bottom of the screen. The white bar at the bottom is shrinking. This is likely a timer or remaining-steps bar.
So the mouse click on the green square didn't change the gameplay objects, only the timer bar.
Let me think about the goal. Maybe I need to click on the colored objects on the left side to change them or match them to the right side.
Let me try clicking on the red shape on the right side.
